debate.title: Large Linear Structure

defs.def: Tully-Fisher relation - A widely verified empirical relationship between the mass or intrinsic luminosity of a spiral galaxy and its asymptotic rotation velocity or emission line width.

Recoil - Merging black holes release a massive amount of energy in the form of gravitational waves, and this can happen anisotropically, kicking the resulting black hole in a direction opposite to the released gravitational waves. A strongly recoiling SMBH can look like a line emerging out of a galaxy.
Globular clusters - Globular clusters are dense groups of tens of thousands to millions of stars, tightly bound by gravity into a spheroidal shape, orbiting galaxies often in their halos.
Bulgeless edge-on galaxy - A disc-shaped galaxy that is viewed edge-on from Earth, making it look like a line in the sky.
Supermassive black hole (SMBH) - An extremely massive black hole, typically millions to billions of solar masses, found at the center of most galaxies.
Velocity gradient - The rate at which the velocity of stars or gas changes with position across an astrophysical object, used to infer its internal dynamics and classify its nature.

ref.quote: Year and title blinded from debate judges.
ref.author: P. D. et al.

ref.quote: Year and title blinded from debate judges.
ref.author: J. S. A. et al.

ref.quote: Year and title blinded from debate judges.
ref.author: M. M. et al.

debate.question:
Here is a picture of a real astrophysical system in different filters. What is the best plausible theoretical model to describe this system?
The best plausible theoretical model to describe this system is a runaway supermassive black hole (SMBH) (). Supermassive black holes are typically found within a few parsecs of the center of galaxies. If this hypothesis were true, the SMBH would have to be located ~40 kiloparsecs away from its host galaxy.
Therefore, you should reject . SMBH recoil can eject SMBHs from the centers of their host galaxy during a merger.
The galaxy shows signs of a recent merger (), which supports the hypothesis that the linear structure could be a trail of star formation left in the wake of a recoiling SMBH. Globular clusters are old, massive and compact structures that travel through the circumgalactic medium (CGM).
If the passage of a black hole through the CGM caused star formation, we would observe similar trails for globular clusters, but in practice we do not. The SMBH in this model is traveling at supersonic speeds along its trajectory.
The bow shock due to this supersonic travel would cause star formation along the way. Globular clusters do not form a bow shock in the CGM, and thus would not have star forming trails. Figure 1 in shows that the color of the stars along the hypothesised wake fluctuates.
If the star formation was caused by the bow shock of a passing SMBH, the age of the stars would monotonically decrease along the structure, but the observed fluctuating color does not support that. While a broad age gradient is expected along the wake, the color fluctuations can be explained by alternating red and blue supergiants in the trail. Star formation will not only occur at the bow shock, but after the passage of the SMBH as well, which would also add fluctuations to the stellar age. Therefore, you should reject .
Therefore, you should reject .
Therefore, you should reject .
Therefore, you should reject .
Therefore, you should reject [None].
The best plausible theoretical model to describe this system is a bulgeless edge-on galaxy (). The line ratio of the linear object ([OIII]/H𝜷) far exceeds the ratio expected from a galaxy. Additionally, the structure is not entirely linear, which would be expected if it were a bulgeless edge-on galaxy.
Therefore, you should reject . The Tully-Fisher relation is used to classify objects as galaxies based on their estimated stellar mass and maximum rotational velocity.
The linear object satisfies the Tully-Fisher relation, making it a strong candidate for a bulgeless edge-on galaxy (Figure 2 in ). Satisfying the Tully-Fisher relation is not sufficient to classify an object as a galaxy given observational inconsistencies.
The velocity gradient near the edge of the object is too large for it to be classified as a galaxy (Figure 1, Figure 5 in ). IC5249 is an observationally confirmed bulgeless edge-on galaxy.
The position vs rotational velocity gradient of the stars in the structure resembles the gradient of IC5249 (Figure 1 in ), suggesting that they're the same class of objects. The velocity and flux gradients of IC5249 appear smoother than those of the object in your figure.
Additionally, the position vs rotational velocity curve you present is only part of the entire linear structure. The full gradient of the object (Figure 10 in ) does not match that of a galaxy. The fitted function that you present here also appears to include a part of the galaxy on the left side, with velocity measurements close to 0 on the far left. Galactic mergers can leave extended trails of star formation between merging galaxies, offering an alternative explanation for the galaxy hypothesis. Therefore, you should reject .
Therefore, you should reject .
Therefore, you should reject .
Therefore, you should reject .
Therefore, you should reject [None].
